title: POS Terminal Tamper and Intrusion Switch Alerts

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI DSS 9.3.2 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PCI DSS 9.3.2 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.59: POS Terminal Tamper and Intrusion Switch Alerts.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.11.59' running on index=pos sourcetype="pos:device_health" — terminal_id, alert_type, severity, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=pos sourcetype="pos:device_health" earliest=-7d
| where match(alert_type,"(?i)tamper|lid|intrusion|case|seal|debug_port")
| lookup pci_store_terminals.csv terminal_id OUTPUT store_id
| stats earliest(_time) as first_alert, latest(_time) as last_alert, values(alert_type) as types by terminal_id, store_id
| sort first_alert
description: Centralizes tamper switches, lid open, and unexpected reboot signals from retail payment terminals so physical device integrity controls for card-present environments are actively monitored.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for PCI DSS compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes pos and sourcetypes pos:device_health. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Map terminal IDs to merchant locations. (2) Correlate with technician dispatch tickets. (3) Page on `severity=CRITICAL`. (4) Monthly store tamper rollup for PCI onsite interviews. Indexes required: `index=pos`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=pos:device_health`; Lookups: `pci_store_terminals`, `pci_store_terminals.csv`.
knownFalsePositives: CDE access from authorized payment ops, scheduled batch reconciliation, or PCI assessor activities.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Custom POS syslog `sourcetype="verifone:vxp"` or `ingenico:telium"` HEC.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=pos` `sourcetype="pos:device_health"` — `terminal_id`, `alert_type`, `severity`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Map terminal IDs to merchant locations. (2) Correlate with technician dispatch tickets. (3) Page on `severity=CRITICAL`. (4) Monthly store tamper rollup for PCI onsite interviews.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=pos sourcetype="pos:device_health" earliest=-7d
| where match(alert_type,"(?i)tamper|lid|intrusion|case|seal|debug_port")
| lookup pci_store_terminals.csv terminal_id OUTPUT store_id
| stats earliest(_time) as first_alert, latest(_time) as last_alert, values(alert_type) as types by terminal_id, store_id
| sort first_alert
```

Understanding this SPL

**POS Terminal Tamper and Intrusion Switch Alerts (PCI DSS Req 9.5.1, 9.3.2)** — Centralizes tamper switches, lid open, and unexpected reboot signals from retail payment terminals so physical device integrity controls for card-present environments are actively monitored.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=pos` `sourcetype="pos:device_health"` — `terminal_id`, `alert_type`, `severity`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Custom POS syslog `sourcetype="verifone:vxp"` or `ingenico:telium"` HEC. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: pos; **sourcetype**: pos:device_health. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=pos, sourcetype="pos:device_health", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Filters the current rows with `where match(alert_type,"(?i)tamper|lid|intrusion|case|seal|debug_port")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by terminal_id, store_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the cardholder data audit log, validate a few expected payment-ops and assessor activities, and confirm events, fields, and index permissions are correct.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Map (store_id), table (terminal_id, types), timeline (alerts).
grandmaExplanation: We centralizes tamper switches, lid open, and unexpected reboot signals from retail payment terminals so physical device integrity controls for card-present environments are actively monitored. PCI DSS is about protecting card and payment data when we run these checks.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Intrusion_Detection.Intrusion_Detection by Intrusion_Detection.signature, Intrusion_Detection.dest, Intrusion_Detection.severity | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Intrusion_Detection (CIM data model).
